headline: Malaysia's flood bill fell 32% in 2025. The State paid more of a smaller bill.
context: On 15 April 2026, the Department of Statistics Malaysia reported total flood damage of RM636.9 million for 2025, down from RM933.4 million in 2024. Inside that smaller total, the share borne by public assets and infrastructure rose.

cards.big: Malaysia's flood damage bill fell from RM933.4M in 2024 to RM636.9M in 2025. Household and business damage fell. Public assets and infrastructure damage rose.
cards.sub: Three of every five ringgit of flood damage in 2025 hit something the State has to rebuild. DOSM released the figures on 15 April 2026.

cards.big: DOSM reported total flood losses of RM636.9M for 2025, down 31.8% from RM933.4M in 2024 and equal to 0.03% of nominal GDP.
cards.sub: The figure covers direct damage categories captured by DOSM's flood loss survey and government agency data, across calendar 2025.

cards.big: Public-asset damage rose 25.3% to RM380.2M in 2025, while household losses fell 50.6% to RM183.8M. Public infrastructure now carries about 60% of the bill.
cards.sub: In 2024 it carried 33%. The State's share grew by RM77 million; household, farm and business damage all fell sharply.

cards.big: DOSM counts direct property and infrastructure damage. Business interruption, productivity loss, deaths and prolonged displacement sit outside the number.
cards.sub: A 2024 World Bank and BNM study put a 1-in-20-year flood at up to 4.1% of GDP by 2030. DOSM's calendar-year survey uses different methodology.

cards.big: The question is not whether floods got cheaper in 2025. It is whose balance sheet the damage that did happen showed up on.
Think of it like a household whose repair bill drops because the public works budget absorbs more of the damage directly.
cards.sub: Less household pain. A larger claim on the public purse. Both can be true in the same year.

cards.big: The damage bill fell by RM296 million. The public infrastructure share rose by RM77 million. The story is in the mix, not the total.
